title: What Is Section 106 of the NHPA? A Complete Guide
content:
Section 106 of the National Historic Preservation Act is one of the most consequential federal laws affecting construction, infrastructure, and land development projects across the United States. Whether you are a federal agency, a state transportation department, or a private developer using federal funding or permits, understanding Section 106 compliance is essential before breaking ground. Failing to meet its requirements can halt projects, trigger legal challenges, and result in costly delays.

Quick Answer: Section 106 of the National Historic Preservation Act (NHPA) requires federal agencies to consider the effects of their undertakings on historic properties before approving or funding a project. The process involves identifying historic resources, assessing potential impacts, and consulting with stakeholders to avoid, minimize, or mitigate adverse effects.

This consultation-driven review process touches thousands of projects each year — from highway expansions and pipeline corridors to housing developments and cell tower installations. Understanding exactly what Section 106 requires, when it applies, and what each step entails helps project proponents plan more effectively and avoid regulatory surprises.

What Does Section 106 of the NHPA Actually Require?

Section 106 requires federal agencies to take into account the effects of their undertakings on historic properties and to afford the Advisory Council on Historic Preservation (ACHP) a reasonable opportunity to comment. This obligation exists before the agency approves, funds, or issues permits for a project — not after.

The law does not prohibit harm to historic properties outright. Instead, it mandates a structured consultation process that brings federal agencies, State Historic Preservation Officers (SHPOs), Tribal Historic Preservation Officers (THPOs), and other stakeholders together to evaluate impacts and explore alternatives. The goal is informed decision-making, not automatic preservation.

The regulations implementing Section 106 are codified at 36 CFR Part 800. These regulations define key terms, outline each step of the review process, and establish timelines for agency and SHPO responses. Familiarity with 36 CFR Part 800 is fundamental for anyone involved in federal project planning.

When Does Section 106 Apply to a Project?

Section 106 review is triggered whenever a federal agency has an undertaking — defined as any project, activity, or program that is funded, licensed, permitted, or carried out by a federal agency. The presence of any federal nexus, no matter how small, can bring a project under Section 106 review.

Common triggers include federal construction projects, federally funded highway and transit improvements, Army Corps of Engineers permits (such as Section 404 wetlands permits), Federal Communications Commission antenna structure registrations, and HUD-funded housing projects. Even a small federal grant can create a Section 106 obligation for an otherwise privately funded development.

Private developers often discover this requirement only after receiving a federal permit or loan guarantee. At that point, the consultation process must be completed before the agency can proceed — underscoring why early identification of the federal nexus is so critical to realistic project scheduling.

What Are the Four Steps of the Section 106 Process?

The Section 106 process follows four sequential steps established by 36 CFR Part 800: initiating the process, identifying historic properties, assessing effects, and resolving adverse effects. Each step involves specific actions and consultation requirements.

Step 1 — Initiate the process: The federal agency identifies the undertaking, its area of potential effects (APE), and the consulting parties, which typically include the SHPO/THPO, federally recognized tribes with cultural interests, and local governments. The agency notifies these parties and invites their participation.

Step 2 — Identify historic properties: The agency, often working with a qualified archaeology and historic preservation firm, conducts background research and field surveys to locate properties that may be eligible for the National Register of Historic Places within the APE. This is where Phase I archaeological surveys and architectural surveys typically occur.

Step 3 — Assess effects: Once historic properties are identified, the agency evaluates whether the undertaking will alter their characteristics — physical, visual, auditory, or atmospheric — in a way that diminishes their integrity or significance. The finding may be "no historic properties affected," "no adverse effect," or "adverse effect."

Step 4 — Resolve adverse effects: When an adverse effect is found, the agency consults with the SHPO/THPO and other parties to develop a Memorandum of Agreement (MOA) or Programmatic Agreement (PA) that outlines mitigation measures, such as archaeological data recovery, historical documentation, or design modifications.

What Is the Area of Potential Effects (APE)?

The area of potential effects is the geographic area within which a project may directly or indirectly cause changes in the character or use of historic properties. Defining the APE correctly is one of the most important — and sometimes contested — decisions in the Section 106 process.

The APE must account for both direct and indirect effects. A highway widening project, for example, has a direct APE encompassing the construction footprint and right-of-way, but it may also have an indirect APE that extends to adjacent neighborhoods where increased noise or visual impacts could affect the integrity of historic districts.

Agencies are expected to consult with the SHPO and other parties when determining the APE. An APE that is drawn too narrowly can lead to challenges later if previously unidentified resources are affected, while an unnecessarily large APE can multiply survey costs and timelines without adding meaningful protection.

How Does Section 106 Differ From NEPA Environmental Review?

Section 106 of the NHPA and the National Environmental Policy Act (NEPA) are complementary but distinct federal review processes. NEPA requires agencies to analyze the environmental impacts of proposed federal actions, including effects on cultural resources, while Section 106 establishes a specific consultation and mitigation framework focused solely on historic properties.

Many projects are subject to both reviews simultaneously. Agencies often integrate the Section 106 process into NEPA documentation to improve efficiency, but the two processes have different legal requirements, timelines, and consulting parties. Meeting NEPA obligations does not automatically satisfy Section 106, and vice versa.

For project proponents, understanding both frameworks — and how they interact — is essential for realistic scheduling. Cultural resources research and survey work often inform both processes, making early coordination with a qualified CRM firm a practical way to reduce duplication and keep projects on track.

Frequently Asked Questions

Who is responsible for conducting Section 106 review?

The federal agency with jurisdiction over the undertaking is legally responsible for conducting Section 106 review. In practice, project proponents — developers, state transportation agencies, or utilities — often manage the day-to-day coordination and hire qualified cultural resources consultants to conduct the required surveys and documentation.

How long does the Section 106 process take?

Timelines vary widely depending on project complexity, the size of the APE, and the density of historic resources. A straightforward undertaking with no historic properties identified can be resolved in weeks. Projects requiring Phase II evaluation, adverse effect findings, and MOA negotiations may take six months to several years.

What qualifies as a historic property under Section 106?

A historic property is any prehistoric or historic district, site, building, structure, or object included in or eligible for inclusion in the National Register of Historic Places. Eligibility is based on the property's age (generally 50+ years), integrity, and significance under one or more of four established National Register criteria.

Can a project proceed if Section 106 identifies an adverse effect?

Yes. An adverse effect finding does not automatically stop a project. The agency must consult with the SHPO, THPO, and other parties to execute a Memorandum of Agreement that identifies mitigation measures — such as archaeological excavation, historic documentation, or design changes — that reduce or offset harm to the affected historic properties.

Do tribes have a role in the Section 106 process?

Yes. Federal agencies must make reasonable and good-faith efforts to identify and consult with federally recognized Indian tribes that may attach religious or cultural significance to historic properties in the APE — even if those properties are not on tribal lands. THPOs have the same consultation standing as SHPOs for properties on tribal land.

Work With an Experienced Section 106 Compliance Partner

Navigating Section 106 of the National Historic Preservation Act requires both regulatory knowledge and on-the-ground fieldwork expertise. From defining the APE and conducting Phase I surveys to preparing effect assessments and negotiating MOAs, each step demands careful attention to federal guidelines and state-specific requirements.

Tennessee Valley Archaeological Research (TVAR) brings over 14 years of CRM experience to Section 106 projects across the Southeast. Our RPA-certified archaeologists and historic preservation specialists have guided federal agencies, state DOTs, and private developers through every phase of the consultation process.
